name: Battle of Kirkuk
description: Imagine you're a Green Beret riding into Kirkuk on the back of a Toyota pickup, wedged between Kurdish Peshmerga fighters who've been waiting thirty years for this moment. April 10, 2003. The Iraqi 5th Corps — tanks, artillery, thousands of troops — just... evaporated. One day they were dug in across the Green Line, the next they were ghosts. ODA 044 and the Peshmerga rolled through positions that should have taken weeks to crack. The oil fields were burning — Saddam's scorched earth — and the black smoke turned noon into dusk. Kirkuk fell in hours, not days. The Peshmerga were weeping in the streets, kissing the ground. For them this was liberation. For the Americans who'd lived with them in the mountains for weeks, eating what they ate, sleeping in the cold — this was the payoff. But here's the thing nobody talks about: the real war in Kirkuk was just beginning.
result: Coalition / Peshmerga victory — Kirkuk captured

name: Thunder Run — Fall of Baghdad
description: April 5, 2003. Colonel Dave Perkins sends a column of M1 Abrams and Bradleys screaming up Highway 8 into the beating heart of Baghdad. Not a probe. Not a raid. A Thunder Run — an armored fist straight into the capital of a country that still had an army. The first run on April 5 was supposed to be a demonstration. They shot their way through six miles of urban hell and came back. But the second run on April 7? Perkins made the call that changed the war: 'We're not coming back out.' They seized Saddam's palaces and dared the entire Iraqi military to take them back. RPGs were skipping off the pavement. Suicide vehicles were ramming into tanks. And the supply line back to the airport was getting cut faster than they could clear it. The guys at the highway interchanges — the Bradleys holding those intersections — they fought for their lives for hours in a 360-degree kill zone. Baghdad fell because a colonel decided that retreating would cost more lives than staying. He was right. But my God, the gamble.
result: Decisive US victory — Baghdad falls

name: Battle of Nasiriyah
description: March 23, 2003. An-Nasiriyah. The Euphrates crossing that was supposed to be a speed bump turned into a meat grinder. The Marines of Task Force Tarawa hit the city and found out real fast that the Fedayeen didn't get the memo about conventional warfare being over. They fought from hospitals, from schools, from rooftops. The 507th Maintenance Company — Jessica Lynch's convoy — had already been ambushed that morning, soldiers killed and captured on roads they were never supposed to be on. Then the Marines pushed in, and an A-10 accidentally strafed their own AAVs at the Saddam Canal bridge. Eighteen Marines killed in a single day. The amtracs were burning. The city was a labyrinth of alleys and fire. Every bridge was contested. This was the moment — just three days into the invasion — when the entire country realized this war was not going to be the clean, surgical thing the briefings promised. Nasiriyah was the wake-up call written in blood.
result: US victory — Euphrates bridges secured

name: Battle of Tikrit
description: Saddam's hometown. The last major city. Every analyst expected the final stand here — the Republican Guard digging in to defend the birthplace of the regime. April 13-15, 2003. The 4th Infantry Division and Special Operations forces pushed north from Baghdad expecting a bloodbath. What they got was stranger: organized resistance that melted into the population. Tikrit fell fast, but it never really fell at all. The fighters just took off their uniforms and went home — with their weapons. The same men who surrendered Tikrit would spend the next eight years planting IEDs and running insurgent cells. Every patrol through those streets for the next decade was haunted by the same question: is that guy a farmer, or is he the Republican Guard officer who buried his AK in the backyard? Tikrit's 'fall' was the beginning of the insurgency, and everyone who served in northern Iraq knows it.
result: US capture — organized resistance dissolves into insurgency

name: Battle of Tal Afar
description: September 2005. Colonel H.R. McMaster — yeah, that McMaster — took his 3rd Armored Cavalry Regiment into Tal Afar and did something almost nobody else managed in the entire war: he actually won. Not just militarily — he won the population. Tal Afar was an al-Qaeda stronghold, a Turkmen city torn apart by sectarian murder between Sunni and Shia neighborhoods. The insurgents had turned it into a factory of car bombs and beheadings. McMaster's approach was different. Clear, hold, build — and he meant it. His troopers went block by block through the ancient castle district, fighting room to room in a fortress that was old when the Ottomans arrived. But then they stayed. They built outposts. They learned names. President Bush called Tal Afar a model for the rest of Iraq. For anyone who served in northern Iraq, Tal Afar was proof that the counterinsurgency theory could work — if you had the right commander, enough troops, and enough time. The tragedy is that most places had none of the three.
result: Coalition victory — city secured, temporary model of COIN success

name: Hawija Security Operations
description: Hawija. If you served in Kirkuk province, you know Hawija. Forty kilometers southwest of Kirkuk city, and it might as well have been another planet. Sunni Arab heartland in a province the Kurds considered theirs. Every patrol into Hawija was a dice roll. The IEDs were relentless — daisy-chained 155mm artillery shells buried in roads that looked exactly the same as the last time you drove them, except now there was a pressure plate under that patch of dirt that wasn't there yesterday. The town was a revolving door: clear it, leave, and the insurgents flowed right back in. Hawija never stopped fighting. In 2013, Iraqi security forces would massacre protesters there, and the town would become one of the first to welcome ISIS with open arms. Everyone who drove those roads between Kirkuk and Hawija carried Hawija with them. The tension in the air. The way the market would go quiet. You learned to read silence in places like that.
result: Ongoing contested security — never fully pacified

name: Kirkuk Province IED Campaign
description: There was no single 'Battle of Kirkuk' after 2003 — there were thousands of them. Every day, every patrol, every convoy. Kirkuk province was an IED laboratory. The bomb makers got better every month. They started with simple pressure plates and graduated to explosively formed penetrators — EFPs — copper discs that Iranian engineering turned into molten jets capable of punching through the side of an Abrams. The roads south toward Hawija, the highways north toward Erbil, the streets of Kirkuk city itself — all of them were wired. You'd see the signs if you knew what to look for: disturbed earth, a dead animal on the shoulder, a piece of trash that wasn't there on the last patrol. Sometimes you didn't see the signs at all. Kirkuk was the fault line — Arabs, Kurds, Turkmen, all fighting over the oil beneath their feet — and every one of those fault lines produced fighters, informants, and bomb makers. For the men and women who ran those routes day after day, Kirkuk wasn't a battle. It was a war of nerves measured in inches of road.
result: Ongoing attritional campaign — thousands of IED incidents

name: Dibis District Operations
description: Dibis sits northwest of Kirkuk along the road to Erbil, straddling the ethnic seam between Kurdish and Arab territories. It was never a headline battle — it was the kind of grinding, daily security operation that defined Kirkuk province. Checkpoints on roads that wound through agricultural land. Patrols through villages where the elders smiled and the young men watched with flat eyes. The oil infrastructure in and around Dibis made it a target — pipeline sabotage was a weekly occurrence, and guarding kilometers of pipe through open terrain was an exercise in futility. Every explosion, every interdicted weapons cache, every detained suspect was a data point in a war that nobody back home was paying attention to. But for the soldiers and airmen pulling security in Dibis district, it was their whole world. The dust, the heat, the boredom that could turn lethal in a heartbeat.
result: Sustained security operations — area remained contested

name: Kirkuk Provincial Reconstruction
description: The guns-and-butter war. Kirkuk province after the surge meant PRTs — Provincial Reconstruction Teams — trying to build a functioning society while people were still shooting at each other. Imagine sitting in a shura with tribal leaders debating sewer contracts while mortars thud in the distance. Imagine signing off on a school renovation knowing the contractor might be paying protection money to the same insurgents who attacked your base last week. This was the other war — the one fought with spreadsheets and handshakes and an interpreter you prayed was translating accurately. Kirkuk's reconstruction was uniquely cursed because Article 140 — the constitutional provision for a referendum on whether Kirkuk would be Kurdish or Iraqi — turned every development dollar into a political weapon. Build a road in a Kurdish neighborhood? The Arabs cry foul. Fund an Arab school? The Kurds accuse you of legitimizing Saddam's Arabization. Nobody who served on reconstruction in Kirkuk province left thinking it was simple.
result: Partial progress — reconstruction complicated by ethnic politics

name: ISIS Seizure of Kirkuk Area
description: June 2014. The Iraqi Army collapsed. Not retreated — collapsed. Divisions that the US had spent a decade training, equipping, and mentoring dropped their weapons and ran. ISIS — the mutant offspring of al-Qaeda in Iraq — swept out of Mosul and poured south. They reached the outskirts of Kirkuk. And then something happened that every veteran of northern Iraq understood in their bones: the Peshmerga stepped in. The Kurdish fighters moved into the vacuum, seized Kirkuk city, and drew a line. They held it, but barely. ISIS fighters were in Hawija — of course they were in Hawija — and the villages south and west of Kirkuk became a no-man's-land. For anyone who'd served in Kirkuk province, watching this unfold on CNN was a special kind of hell. Those roads you drove. Those checkpoints you manned. Those markets where you bought chai. All of it overrun by a medieval death cult wearing black flags. The places you bled to secure, lost in a week. That's the part that keeps you up at night.
result: Kirkuk city held by Peshmerga — surrounding areas contested

name: Liberation of Hawija
description: October 2017. Three years after ISIS turned Hawija into a stronghold, Iraqi forces finally came to take it back. This was one of the last major ISIS urban positions in Iraq, and the jihadists knew it. They'd had three years to dig in, to mine every road, to rig every building. The Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service and Army divisions pushed in from multiple axes, supported by coalition airstrikes that turned whole blocks to rubble. The civilians who emerged were ghosts — starved, traumatized, some of them having lived under ISIS rule since 2014. For veterans of Kirkuk province, Hawija's liberation was bittersweet in a way that's hard to explain. The town that had been a thorn in your side for years, the place where you lost friends to IEDs, the district that never stopped resisting — now it was being 'liberated' from a group that made the old insurgents look like amateurs. The cycle of violence in Hawija stretches back to 2003, and anyone honest will tell you it didn't end in 2017 either.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Coalition air support
result: Iraqi victory — Hawija liberated after three years of ISIS control

name: First Battle of Fallujah (Operation Vigilant Resolve)
description: April 2004. Four Blackwater contractors get killed, burned, and hung from a bridge over the Euphrates. The images go worldwide. The Marines are ordered to take Fallujah. They push in hard — house to house, block to block — and they're winning. The insurgents are getting ground up. And then the order comes: stop. Political pressure, Al Jazeera footage of civilian casualties, Iraqi Governing Council members threatening to quit. The Marines are pulled back. They hand the city to the 'Fallujah Brigade' — a hastily assembled unit of former Iraqi soldiers — who immediately hand their weapons to the insurgents. Every Marine who fought in that first battle carries the same bitter knowledge: they had Fallujah, and politicians gave it back. The city became the capital of the insurgency, a bomb factory, an al-Qaeda headquarters. And everyone knew they'd have to go back in and do it again. Seven months later, they did.
result: Inconclusive — Marines withdraw, city returned to insurgent control

name: Second Battle of Fallujah (Operation Phantom Fury)
description: November 7, 2004. This time there would be no stopping. Operation Phantom Fury was the largest urban battle since Hue City in 1968. Twelve thousand US troops, principally Marines and Army, plus two thousand Iraqi soldiers, going into a city that had spent seven months turning itself into a fortress. The insurgents had fortified mosques, mined streets, created kill zones in every alley. The Marines breached from the north and attacked south — methodical, brutal, relentless. They fought through buildings rigged to explode. They cleared rooms where fighters were chained to their positions because their leaders didn't trust them not to run. Ninety-five Americans killed. Over a thousand wounded. The city was effectively destroyed. And here's the part that hits different when you've been there: Fallujah was rebuilt. And then ISIS took it in 2014. And it had to be liberated again. The same streets. The same buildings. The same sacrifice. War has a way of making you pay for the same ground twice.
belligerents: US Marines / US Army / Iraqi Forces
result: Decisive Coalition victory — city secured at enormous cost
commanders: LTG John Sattler / COL Craig Tucker

name: Battle of Ramadi
description: 2006. Ramadi, capital of Anbar province, was the most dangerous city on earth. Al-Qaeda in Iraq had declared it the capital of their Islamic State. US forces were taking contact every single day — snipers, IEDs, complex ambushes, VBIEDs. Colonel Sean MacFarland and the Ready First Brigade did something that the analysts said was impossible: they built combat outposts inside the city. Not behind blast walls on a FOB — inside the neighborhoods, on the streets where the fighters lived. It was a calculated bet that proximity would win the population. Every outpost construction was a battle. Every resupply was a fight. The SEALs lost Marc Lee and Mike Monsoor — Monsoor threw himself on a grenade to save his teammates and earned the Medal of Honor. But the Anbar Awakening started here. The tribal sheikhs who'd been sitting on the fence watched the Americans refuse to leave and decided to bet on the winning side. Ramadi flipped. The model that won Ramadi became the template for the surge.
belligerents: US 1st BCT, 1st AD / Iraqi Army / Tribal forces
result: Coalition / Tribal victory — Anbar Awakening begins

name: Battles of Sadr City
description: Sadr City. Two million Shia crammed into a Baghdad slum named after Muqtada al-Sadr's martyred father. The Mahdi Army — Jaish al-Mahdi, JAM — owned these streets. In 2004, they rose up and fought the US military to a standstill. In 2008, they did it again. The second battle was worse. Iranian-supplied EFPs and rockets were raining down on the Green Zone from launch sites in Sadr City. The US and Iraqi response was Operation Gold Wall — they literally built a concrete wall across the southern edge of the district, block by block, under fire, to cut off the rocket teams. Imagine standing behind a crane operator placing T-walls while snipers shoot at you from apartment buildings. Imagine being the crane operator. The fighting in those tight streets — Bradleys and Abrams navigating alleys designed for donkey carts, Predator drones guiding fire onto rooftops — was some of the most intense urban combat of the entire war. Sadr City was the war's reminder that Sunni insurgents weren't the only enemy.
result: Coalition / Iraqi government victory — Mahdi Army ceasefire

name: Battle of Basra (Charge of the Knights)
description: March 2008. Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki personally flew to Basra and ordered the Iraqi Army to take back Iraq's second-largest city from Shia militias. No coordination with the Americans. Minimal planning. It almost ended in disaster. Iraqi units broke and ran. Soldiers were stripping off their uniforms. The Mahdi Army was counterattacking. US and British forces had to scramble air support and advisors to keep the operation from becoming a catastrophe. But then — and this is the part that mattered — the Iraqi forces rallied. Over the next weeks, with coalition support, they pushed the militias out. Maliki's gamble paid off, barely. For the coalition, Basra was a test: could the Iraqi military stand on its own? The honest answer was: not yet, but maybe someday. For the troops who'd spent years training Iraqi battalions, watching their students fight and sometimes flee, Basra was every emotion at once — pride, frustration, fear, and a fragile hope.
result: Iraqi government victory — militias driven from Basra
commanders: PM Nouri al-Maliki / LTG Mohan al-Furayji

name: Battle of Mosul (2004)
description: November 2004. While the world watched Fallujah, the insurgents pulled the rug out from under Mosul. Coordinated attacks hit every police station in the city simultaneously. The Iraqi Police force — 4,000 strong — collapsed overnight. Officers didn't just abandon their posts; they handed their weapons and stations to the insurgents. The US had drawn down forces in Mosul to support Fallujah, and the skeleton crew left behind watched the city unravel in real time. Stryker brigades had to fight to retake a city they'd already secured once. For anyone who served in northern Iraq, the fall of Mosul in 2004 was the dress rehearsal for the fall of Mosul in 2014. Same city, same pattern, same sickening feeling of watching institutions you built disintegrate. The lesson was there for anyone willing to learn it: you can't secure a city with forces that don't believe in what they're fighting for.
result: Initial insurgent success — US forces eventually restore control

name: Battle of Mosul (Liberation from ISIS)
description: October 2016 to July 2017. Nine months. The longest urban battle since World War II. Mosul was ISIS's crown jewel — the city where al-Baghdadi declared his caliphate from the Great Mosque of al-Nuri. Retaking it required 100,000 Iraqi, Kurdish, and militia fighters backed by massive coalition air power. The eastern half fell relatively quickly. The western half — the Old City, with its narrow streets and ancient buildings — was a nightmare. ISIS had tunneled under the entire district. They used civilians as shields. They sent VBIEDs — car bombs — in waves, sometimes ten a day. The Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service, the Golden Division, took 40% casualties. Forty percent. The coalition flattened entire neighborhoods with airstrikes. The Great Mosque was destroyed. When it was over, parts of western Mosul looked like Stalingrad. For every OIF veteran watching from home, Mosul's liberation was a hall of mirrors — the same city, the same streets, fought over again by a new generation paying the same price.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Kurdish Peshmerga / Coalition
result: Iraqi / Coalition victory — Mosul liberated, massive destruction

name: Northern Iraq Green Line Offensive
description: March-April 2003. The northern front that almost wasn't. Turkey refused to let the 4th Infantry Division cross its territory, so the entire plan for a two-front invasion collapsed. What replaced it was a handful of Special Forces ODAs, CIA paramilitary teams, and thousands of Kurdish Peshmerga fighters facing down thirteen Iraqi divisions. That's not a typo — thirteen divisions. The plan was audacious to the point of insanity: use precision airstrikes and small-unit maneuver to convince the Iraqis they were facing a much larger force. It worked. The combination of JDAM strikes on ridge-line positions and Peshmerga ground assaults broke the Iraqi defensive lines along the Green Line — the old ceasefire boundary between Kurdish and Iraqi-controlled territory. One by one, the fortified positions fell: Ain Sifni, the Derbendikhan pass, the ridges above the Kirkuk plain. By the time the 173rd Airborne parachuted into Bashur airfield, the northern front was already decided by a few hundred Americans and tens of thousands of Kurds who'd been waiting a generation for this fight.
belligerents: US Special Forces / CIA / Kurdish Peshmerga / 173rd Airborne
result: Coalition / Peshmerga victory — northern Iraqi front collapses

name: Battle of Samarra
description: October 2004. Operation Baton Rouge. Samarra had been an insurgent stronghold for months — the golden-domed al-Askari Mosque making it both strategically vital and politically untouchable. The 1st Infantry Division and Iraqi forces went in hard, and for once, the operation went roughly according to plan. Five thousand troops cleared the city in a week of house-to-house fighting. But Samarra's true significance came later: in February 2006, al-Qaeda bombed the al-Askari Mosque, blowing the golden dome to pieces, and Iraq's sectarian civil war ignited like gasoline. That bombing was the match that lit the fire. Shia death squads went hunting. Sunni neighborhoods emptied. Baghdad was ethnically cleansed block by block. Everything that happened in Iraq from 2006 to 2008 — the surge, the Awakening, the 4,000 American dead — traces back to that mosque in Samarra. The city that the 1st ID cleared became the detonator for the entire country.
belligerents: US 1st Infantry Division / Iraqi National Guard
result: Tactical coalition victory — city cleared but later site of catastrophic mosque bombing

name: Shindand Airbase Operations
description: Shindand. If you've never heard of it, you weren't in western Afghanistan. A sprawling airbase in Herat province, originally built by the Soviets, expanded by the Americans, surrounded by terrain that looked like Mars with scattered mud-brick villages. FOB Shindand was the hub for everything happening in RC-West — the Italian-led regional command that was supposed to be the 'quiet' part of Afghanistan. It wasn't quiet. The base took indirect fire regularly. The roads outside the wire were seeded with IEDs. The local power dynamics were a nested set of Russian dolls — warlords, tribal elders, Afghan National Army units of questionable loyalty, and Taliban shadow governors who ran their own parallel government. Living at Shindand meant existing in a reality that the news never covered. The western front of a war that the public had already forgotten. You breathed moon dust, you sweated through 120-degree summers, you pulled missions into valleys where the terrain itself was the enemy. Shindand wasn't famous. But for the people who served there, it was everything.
belligerents: US Forces / ISAF / Afghan National Security Forces
result: Ongoing operations — base served as key hub for western Afghanistan

name: Shindand Airstrike Incident
description: August 22, 2008. Azizabad village, Shindand district. A US Special Operations raid targeting a Taliban commander called in airstrikes on a compound. The initial report said 30 militants killed. Then the real numbers started coming in. The Afghan government said 90 civilians dead, including 60 children. The UN investigation confirmed the mass civilian casualties. A cell phone video showed rows of small bodies laid out in a courtyard. This was the incident that almost broke the western Afghanistan mission. President Karzai was furious. The Italians running RC-West were horrified. The rules of engagement changed. The relationship between ISAF and the local population — already fragile — shattered in Shindand district. For anyone who later served at Shindand airbase, the shadow of Azizabad was everywhere. The distrust in the eyes of village elders. The protests at the gate. The knowledge that every tactical decision carried the weight of what had happened before you arrived. Azizabad is the reason 'civilian casualties' isn't an abstract concept to anyone who served in Herat province. It has names. It has a village. It has a cell phone video.
result: Tactical success but strategic disaster — mass civilian casualties

name: Battle of Shindand
description: The weeks following the Azizabad airstrike saw Shindand district erupt. Taliban fighters — emboldened by the civilian casualty backlash and the resulting restrictions on air support — launched coordinated attacks on Afghan and coalition outposts throughout the district. It was a calculated move: exploit the political fallout while ISAF was on its heels. Afghan police checkpoints were overrun. Roads were mined. The Italian PRT in Herat and US forces at Shindand airbase had to mount a sustained clearing operation through terrain that the enemy knew intimately — dry riverbeds, compound walls, grape rows that provided perfect concealment. The fighting was close-range and ugly. And every engagement came with a secondary consideration that the Taliban didn't have to worry about: is there a family in that compound? Is that compound clear? Because after Azizabad, one more mistake in Shindand district would be the end of whatever local support remained.
belligerents: US Forces / Italian ISAF / Afghan National Army
result: Coalition restored security — lasting trust damage with local population

name: Herat Province Operations
description: 2010-2012. Western Afghanistan. The part of the war nobody stateside talked about because Helmand and Kandahar were eating all the headlines and all the casualties. But Herat province had its own war — quieter, more complex, arguably more corrupt. The Italian-led Regional Command West ran the show, with US forces providing the sharp end when things got bad. And things got bad more often than the Italians wanted to admit. The Taliban had shadow governors in every district. The drug trade — opium from Farah and Nimroz provinces flowing through Herat to Iran — funded everything. The Afghan Local Police program was a double-edged sword: armed villagers who might fight the Taliban or might use their weapons to settle tribal grudges. For Americans at Shindand and the scattered bases across western Afghanistan, 2011-2012 was the war in its twilight — the surge in the south was winding down, the transition to Afghan lead was the official narrative, and everyone on the ground knew the Taliban was just waiting. Doing their pushups, as the saying went.
belligerents: US Forces / Italian ISAF / Afghan National Security Forces
result: Security gradually transferred to Afghan forces — contested stability

name: Bala Murghab District Operations
description: Bala Murghab. The Wild West of western Afghanistan — and that's saying something in a country where 'wild' is the baseline. This district in Badghis province, northeast of Shindand, was so remote and so contested that resupplying the outposts required air assault operations. The Spanish contingent held positions here that were under near-constant harassment. In 2009-2010, the Taliban effectively controlled most of the district outside the main base. Italian and Spanish ISAF forces, along with US enablers and Afghan troops, launched repeated clearing operations through the Murghab River valley — a green zone of irrigated farmland perfect for ambushes. The operations were grinding, repetitive affairs: push through, take contact, clear compounds, pull back to base, repeat next month. Bala Murghab was the kind of place that chewed through units and morale. Too remote for anyone to care about, too dangerous to ignore. For anyone who flew missions or ran logistics out of Shindand supporting these operations, Bala Murghab was the definition of a forgotten fight.
belligerents: Spanish ISAF / Italian forces / US enablers / Afghan Army
result: Contested — district remained unstable throughout ISAF presence

name: Zerkoh Valley Operations
description: The Zerkoh Valley in Ghor province — east of Shindand, accessible through mountain passes that might as well have been on another planet. This was Taliban transit territory: fighters, weapons, and opium moving between the southern and northern fronts through valleys that Alexander the Great would have recognized. Coalition operations here were sporadic and usually kinetic — you didn't go into the Zerkoh Valley for hearts and minds. You went in because intelligence said a weapons cache or a commander was there, and you hit fast and got out before the terrain ate you. Helicopter support was critical and often unavailable because of the altitude and weather. The Afghan National Army units that partnered on these missions ranged from capable to catastrophic depending on the unit and the day. For the Americans supporting these operations from Shindand, the Zerkoh Valley was the definition of expeditionary warfare in its rawest form — small teams, thin support, and mountain terrain that didn't care about your timeline.
result: Disruption operations — temporary effects on insurgent logistics

name: Farah Province Operations
description: Farah province. South of Herat, bordering Iran and Helmand. If Herat was the 'manageable' part of western Afghanistan, Farah was where things fell apart. In May 2009, Taliban fighters overran parts of Farah city — the provincial capital — in a brazen daylight assault that exposed just how thin ISAF's presence was in the west. US airstrikes to retake the city killed dozens of civilians, adding Farah to the growing list of places where the cure was as damaging as the disease. The province was a drug corridor, a Taliban staging area, and an Iranian influence zone all at once. Operations out of Shindand regularly extended into Farah province — aerial ISR, quick reaction forces, advise-and-assist missions with Afghan units that sometimes needed more assistance than advising. Farah was the evidence that the 'economy of force' approach to western Afghanistan was a polite way of saying 'we don't have enough troops and we're hoping for the best.'
belligerents: US Forces / ISAF / Afghan Security Forces
result: Provincial capital retaken — province remained heavily contested
commanders: ISAF RC-West / US task force commanders

name: Italian ISAF Operations in RC-West
description: The Italians ran Regional Command West, and working with them was an education in coalition warfare — which is a diplomatic way of saying it was complicated. Italy had different rules of engagement, different political constraints, and a fundamentally different appetite for risk than the Americans. Their PRT in Herat city did solid development work. Their Carabinieri trained Afghan police. But when it came to offensive operations, the Italian national caveats — restrictions placed by Rome on what their forces could and couldn't do — created friction that everyone felt but nobody could officially acknowledge. The Americans at Shindand needed Italian coordination for operations in Herat province, and sometimes that coordination came fast and sometimes it didn't come at all. This isn't a knock on Italian soldiers — many of them were excellent, and they took casualties. It's the reality of coalition warfare: every nation has its own politics, its own red lines, its own definition of acceptable risk. The war in western Afghanistan was fought as much in coordination meetings as it was on the ground.
belligerents: Italian ISAF forces / US Forces / ANSF
result: Mixed — effective development work constrained by national caveats

name: Battle of Tora Bora
description: December 2001. Osama bin Laden is trapped in a cave complex in the White Mountains of eastern Afghanistan, and the United States has a choice: send in American troops to finish the job, or rely on Afghan militia commanders who have their own agendas. They chose wrong. The CIA and Special Forces teams on the ground — Gary Berntsen's Jawbreaker and the Delta Force operators — called for a battalion of Rangers to seal the Pakistani border. The request was denied. Instead, Afghan warlords were paid to assault the caves while B-52s dropped the largest conventional bombs in the American arsenal. Bin Laden walked out through the back door into Pakistan. He wouldn't be found for another decade. Tora Bora is the original sin of the Afghan war — the moment where the man responsible for 9/11 slipped through fingers that should have been closed into a fist. Every year of the war that followed, every name on every memorial wall — part of the cost of that decision echoes forward through all of it.
belligerents: US Special Forces / CIA / Afghan militias
result: Al-Qaeda fighters killed but bin Laden escapes to Pakistan

name: Operation Anaconda (Shah-i-Kot Valley)
description: March 2002. The Shahi-Kot Valley. Operation Anaconda was supposed to be the hammer-and-anvil destruction of regrouped al-Qaeda fighters. The plan looked clean on the briefing slides. The reality was a catastrophe of coordination. Afghan militia forces designated as the 'hammer' broke and ran at first contact. The 10th Mountain and 101st Airborne troops who were the 'anvil' suddenly found themselves in a three-sided ambush at the bottom of a valley, taking fire from positions above them that intelligence said didn't exist. Chinooks trying to insert Rangers onto the ridgeline were shot to pieces — the fight on Takur Ghar mountain, Roberts Ridge, became its own legend of heroism and horror. Seven Americans killed in the first hours. The air support that was supposed to prep the battlefield never materialized due to a FUBAR of interservice communication. Anaconda was the battle that taught the Afghan war its real shape: the enemy was tougher than anyone admitted, the terrain was the deadliest weapon in the valley, and coalition warfare is only as strong as its weakest link.
result: Coalition victory — al-Qaeda forces dispersed at significant cost

name: Battle of Wanat
description: July 13, 2008. Vehicle Patrol Base Kahler. Wanat, Nuristan province. Forty-eight American paratroopers from the 173rd Airborne — the same unit that jumped into northern Iraq — were building a new outpost in a village at the bottom of a valley. They'd been there two days. The Taliban hit them at dawn with 200 fighters who'd infiltrated to within hand-grenade range. The attack on the observation post at the top of the hill was so intense that at one point, nine Americans were fighting off attackers from three sides simultaneously. The barrel of an M240 melted. An M2 .50-cal jammed. The TOW missile launcher was destroyed. Nine Americans were killed. Twenty-seven wounded. Out of forty-eight. That's a casualty rate that belongs in a World War I history book. The investigation afterward found failures in planning, in resourcing, in intelligence. But on that hill, on that morning, what happened was young men fighting with the raw courage of people who have no choice. Wanat became a case study taught at every military school in America. The soldiers who survived lived the textbook.
result: US forces held but position abandoned — 9 KIA, 27 WIA

name: Battle of Kamdesh (COP Keating)
description: October 3, 2009. Combat Outpost Keating. Nuristan province. At the bottom of a valley surrounded by mountains on three sides, in a position that every soldier who served there knew was suicidal. They'd been trying to close it for months. The Taliban didn't wait. Three hundred fighters attacked at dawn from the high ground — RPGs, machine guns, recoilless rifles, mortars — pouring fire down into a fishbowl. The Americans were overrun. The Taliban breached the outer perimeter and were inside the outpost, fighting among the buildings. Staff Sergeant Clinton Romesha and Specialist Ty Carter — both later awarded the Medal of Honor — led counterattacks that pushed the enemy back. Close air support saved the outpost, but barely. Eight Americans killed, twenty-five wounded. COP Keating was closed within days — the mission it supported no longer justified the cost. The battle became a symbol of everything wrong with the small-outpost strategy in Afghanistan's valleys: isolated positions, inadequate resources, and soldiers paying with their lives for terrain that had no strategic value. The book 'The Outpost' tells their story. It should be required reading.
result: US forces held — outpost immediately abandoned, 8 KIA
commanders: CPT Stoney Portis / SSG Clinton Romesha

name: Battle of Marjah (Operation Moshtarak)
description: February 2010. Operation Moshtarak — 'Together' in Dari. Fifteen thousand ISAF and Afghan troops hitting Marjah, a farming district in central Helmand that had become the Taliban's economic engine and the world's largest opium bazaar. General McChrystal announced the operation in advance — deliberately — to give civilians time to leave and to signal that this time, coalition forces were coming to stay. The Marines went in by helicopter assault and ground advance into a district laced with IEDs so thick that some companies were hitting two or three a day. The fighting was house to house, compound to compound, through irrigation ditches and poppy fields. McChrystal promised a 'government in a box' — ready to deploy once the Marines cleared the area. The government in a box never really materialized. The district governor was a former convict. The Afghan police were predatory. Marjah became the test case for counterinsurgency theory, and the results were ambiguous at best. The Marines cleared and held with extraordinary courage. What came after the clearing was the part nobody could figure out.
belligerents: US Marines / British Forces / Afghan National Army
result: Coalition military victory — governance remained fragile

name: Battles of Sangin
description: Sangin. The name itself is a eulogy. This district in northern Helmand consumed British and American lives at a rate that made it the most dangerous place in Afghanistan for most of the war. The British took Sangin in 2006 expecting a peacekeeping mission and found themselves in a siege. Their platoon houses in the district center were under daily attack for months. When the US Marines took over in 2010, they inherited a district where the Taliban had freedom of movement, the IED threat was off the charts, and every patrol was a potential mass casualty event. The 3rd Battalion, 5th Marines — 'Darkhorse' — lost 25 killed in their first deployment to Sangin. Twenty-five. In one battalion. In one district. More Marines lost legs in Sangin than any other place in Afghanistan. The prosthetics ward at Walter Reed became a Sangin reunion. For anyone who served in Afghanistan, Sangin is the word that ends arguments about whether the war was 'winding down.' In Sangin, the war was always at its worst.
belligerents: British Forces / US Marines / Afghan Army
result: Eventually transferred to Afghan control — remained contested

name: Korengal Valley Operations
description: The Valley of Death. Six miles long, hemmed in by ridges, home to Korengali tribesmen who didn't like the Afghan government, didn't like the Taliban, and definitely didn't like Americans. From 2005 to 2010, US forces maintained outposts in the Korengal that were in contact almost daily. The 2nd Battalion, 503rd Infantry — 'The Rock' — fought here. The battles were documented in 'Restrepo' and 'Korengal,' Sebastian Junger's films that showed America what the war actually looked like: young men in a remote outpost, fighting every day, losing friends, and struggling to understand why they were there. The Korengal ate units alive. The terrain was so steep that resupply by ground was nearly impossible. Firefights lasted hours. The enemy knew every fold in the ground. In April 2010, the US pulled out. Abandoned the valley entirely. The message was clear: the Korengal wasn't worth the blood. For the families of the men killed there, that's a sentence that will never sit right. Twenty-four-year-olds died for a valley that was given back.
result: US withdrawal in 2010 — valley returned to insurgent control

name: Attack on Camp Bastion
description: September 14, 2012. Fifteen Taliban fighters in US Army uniforms cut through the perimeter wire of Camp Bastion — the massive British/American base in Helmand province — and went straight for the flight line. They destroyed six AV-8B Harrier jump jets. Six. That's more than $200 million in aircraft, and it was the greatest loss of American military aircraft in a single incident since the Tet Offensive in 1968. Two Marines were killed, including Lieutenant Colonel Christopher Raible, the squadron commander, who charged into the fight to defend his aircraft and his Marines. The Taliban had rehearsed the assault for weeks, using a mockup of the base. They knew exactly where to go. The breach exposed a complacency that had crept into base security — the assumption that the big FOBs were untouchable. Prince Harry was on the base at the time, which made international headlines, but the real story was simpler and more devastating: the Taliban walked onto the largest coalition base in southern Afghanistan and wreaked havoc. In 2012. Eleven years into the war.
result: Attack repelled — 6 Harriers destroyed, 2 Marines KIA

name: Tongo Tongo Ambush
description: October 4, 2017. Tongo Tongo, Niger. A twelve-man ODA from 3rd Special Forces Group and thirty Nigerien soldiers were returning from a reconnaissance mission near the Mali border when they drove into a kill zone. Fifty to one hundred ISIS-Greater Sahara fighters hit them from multiple positions in a treeline. No armored vehicles. No air support — the nearest was over an hour away. No quick reaction force. The team split under fire. Four Green Berets were killed: Staff Sergeant Bryan Black, Staff Sergeant Jeremiah Johnson, Staff Sergeant Dustin Wright, and Sergeant La David Johnson. La David Johnson was separated from the group and found 48 hours later, nearly a mile from the ambush site. He'd fought alone. The ambush exposed what most Americans didn't know: we had troops in Niger. We had troops fighting and dying in West Africa, in a war against ISIS affiliates that Congress hadn't debated and the public hadn't been told about. Four families got the worst knock on their door, and America briefly asked 'wait — we're in Niger?' before moving on. The dead don't get to move on.
belligerents: US 3rd SFG ODA 3212 / Nigerien Armed Forces
result: US/Niger forces ambushed — 4 US KIA, 4 Nigerien KIA

name: Niger-Mali Border Operations
description: The Niger-Mali border region. Sand, scrub, and scattered villages in terrain so vast and so empty that controlling it is more concept than reality. After Tongo Tongo, the operations didn't stop — they got more cautious, more heavily supported, but they continued. US Special Forces and conventional enablers worked alongside Nigerien and French forces in a part of the world where the 'war on terror' looked nothing like Iraq or Afghanistan. No FOBs. No paved roads. No satellite TV in the chow hall. Just small teams living at austere outposts, driving unarmored Toyotas, advising partner forces whose capabilities ranged from professional to non-existent. The threat was ISIS-Greater Sahara to the west and Boko Haram to the southeast, with al-Qaeda affiliates scattered everywhere in between. For anyone deployed to Niger in 2017-2018, the mission was real, the danger was real, and the disconnect between what you were doing and what anyone back home understood about it was total. Africa was the forgotten theater of a forgotten war.
belligerents: US Special Forces / Nigerien Armed Forces / French Forces
result: Ongoing security operations — partners trained, area remained contested

name: Operation Barkhane (French Sahel Operations)
description: The French ran the biggest counterterrorism operation in the Sahel, and if you were deployed to Niger, you worked alongside them. Operation Barkhane — 4,500 French troops spread across five countries, operating from bases in N'Djamena, Niamey, and Gao. The French Foreign Legion and Marine Infantry units were the sharp end, running long-range desert patrols and helicopter assaults against jihadi groups in terrain that makes Afghanistan look lush. They killed senior leaders. They disrupted cells. They fought gun battles in the sand dunes of northern Mali. And they were losing the war. The Sahel was getting worse every year — more groups, more attacks, more territory lost to jihadists. For the Americans working alongside Barkhane, it was a masterclass in the limits of military force: the French were tactically superb and strategically stuck. You can't kill your way out of a problem rooted in state collapse, poverty, and ethnic grievance. By 2022, the French would be kicked out of Mali by a military junta that preferred Russian mercenaries. The whole thing unraveled. But in 2017-2018, the partnership was real, the missions were real, and the French bled alongside us.
belligerents: French Armed Forces / Sahel partner nations
ISGS / JNIM / Ansar Dine / various jihadi groups
result: Ongoing operations — tactical successes but strategic deterioration

name: Battle of Konna
description: January 2013. The jihadists controlling northern Mali pushed south — toward Bamako, the capital. Konna was the last town before the line of no return. If Konna fell, the Malian military had nothing between the jihadists and a capital city of two million people. The Malian army fought and lost. Konna fell in hours. The Malian government begged France for help, and Paris responded with Operation Serval — Mirage fighters, helicopters, paratroopers, and Foreign Legionnaires flooding into Mali in a matter of days. French helicopter attacks stopped the jihadi advance at Konna. It was the opening shot of France's Sahel war, the intervention that would eventually become Operation Barkhane, and the reason there were American forces in Niger when Tongo Tongo happened. Konna was the moment the world realized that the collapse of Libya in 2011 had flooded the Sahel with weapons and fighters, and that a new front in the war on terror had opened in the vast emptiness of West Africa.
belligerents: Malian Armed Forces / French Operation Serval
result: Jihadi advance halted by French intervention
commanders: Iyad Ag Ghali (Ansar Dine) / AQIM leaders

name: Battle of Gao
description: February 2013. French and Malian forces liberated Gao — the largest city in northern Mali — from the jihadist coalition that had held it for nine months. MUJAO fighters had imposed sharia law, amputated hands, stoned people, and destroyed anything they considered un-Islamic. The French retook the city quickly, but the jihadists didn't leave — they switched to guerrilla warfare. Car bombs, suicide attacks, and infiltration into the city from the surrounding desert. A car bomb at a checkpoint. A suicide attacker at the police station. The battle for Gao wasn't a single fight; it was the transition from conventional liberation to the asymmetric grind that would define the Sahel conflict for the next decade. For anyone who deployed to the region, Gao was the proof that taking territory back was the easy part. Holding it — governing it — that's where wars are won or lost. The Sahel hasn't figured that out yet.
result: City liberated — ongoing guerrilla resistance

name: Boko Haram — Lake Chad Basin Operations
description: The Lake Chad Basin — where Nigeria, Niger, Chad, and Cameroon meet — became Boko Haram's killing ground. By 2015, Boko Haram controlled territory the size of Belgium, had killed tens of thousands, displaced over two million people, and kidnapped 276 girls from Chibok that the whole world tweeted about and then forgot. The Multinational Joint Task Force — Nigerian, Nigerien, Chadian, and Cameroonian soldiers with US, French, and British support — launched a grinding campaign to retake the region. The fighting was brutal: Boko Haram used child suicide bombers, slaughtered entire villages, and fought with a savagery that shocked even hardened soldiers. For Americans deployed to Niger, the Boko Haram threat to the southeast was the other war — the one happening just across the border from where you slept. The explosions you sometimes heard weren't thunder. The refugees streaming through your area of operations weren't fleeing drought. The Lake Chad conflict was and remains one of the worst humanitarian disasters on earth, and almost nobody in America could find it on a map.
result: Boko Haram territory reduced but group not defeated — ongoing

name: Fall of Kabul
description: August 15, 2021. Twenty years, two trillion dollars, 2,461 American dead, over 100,000 Afghan casualties — and the Taliban walked into Kabul without a fight. President Ghani fled. The Afghan National Army, 300,000 strong on paper, dissolved like sugar in water. Taliban fighters — many of them not yet born on 9/11 — sat in the presidential palace and posed for photos. At Hamid Karzai International Airport, the most desperate images of the 21st century played out: thousands of Afghans crushing against the gates, climbing the walls, clinging to C-17 landing gear as planes took off and falling to their deaths. Babies passed over razor wire. A suicide bomber — ISIS-K — killed 13 US service members and 170 Afghans at Abbey Gate. For every person who served in Afghanistan — who patrolled those streets, mentored those soldiers, drank chai with those elders — August 2021 was a wound that hasn't closed. You watched the country you bled for collapse in eleven days. You watched the people who trusted you scramble over walls to escape the people you couldn't defeat. The question 'was it worth it?' isn't rhetorical anymore. It's personal.
belligerents: Afghan National Security Forces / US Forces (withdrawal)
result: Taliban seize Kabul — Islamic Emirate restored

name: Abbey Gate Bombing (Kabul Airport)
description: August 26, 2021. Abbey Gate, Hamid Karzai International Airport. A suicide bomber from ISIS-Khorasan walked into the crush of thousands of desperate Afghans trying to get into the airport and detonated. Thirteen American service members killed — eleven Marines, a Navy corpsman, and a soldier. The youngest was 20 years old. One hundred seventy Afghan civilians died beside them. The Marines at Abbey Gate knew the threat was coming — intelligence had warned of an imminent ISIS-K attack — but the mission required them to stand in that crowd, processing evacuees, patting down desperate people handing them babies. They couldn't close the gate because closing the gate meant abandoning the people they'd promised to save. So they stood there. In the blast zone. Doing their jobs. Lance Corporal Rylee McCollum, 20, expecting his first child. Corporal Humberto Sanchez, 22. Staff Sergeant Darin Taylor Hoover, 31. Thirteen names that represent the final American blood price of a twenty-year war. They died doing what soldiers have always done: standing between the vulnerable and the violence, even when the mission was already lost.
result: 13 US service members KIA, 170+ Afghan civilians killed

name: Battles of Panjwai District
description: Panjwai district, Kandahar province. The Taliban's birthplace. The grape rows and pomegranate orchards where Mullah Omar built his movement. Canadians fought here first in 2006 — Operation Medusa was the biggest conventional battle NATO had fought since Korea, and it nearly broke the Canadian military. The Taliban stood and fought in the green zone, and the Canadians, later reinforced by US forces, ground them down in close combat among mud-brick walls and irrigation canals. But Panjwai never stopped. Year after year, unit after unit — Canadians, Americans, Afghans — cleared the same compounds, walked the same trails, stepped on the same pressure-plate IEDs. The district was so mined that some units had more amputees from Panjwai than from anywhere else in the entire war. The grape rows — dense, chest-high walls of dried mud supporting grape vines — were natural fortifications. You couldn't see through them, you couldn't drive through them, and the Taliban used them like trench lines. Panjwai was the war distilled to its essence: an enemy that wouldn't leave, fighting in terrain that favored the defender, in a war where 'winning' was never clearly defined.
belligerents: Canadian Forces / US Forces / Afghan Army
result: Repeated clearing operations — never fully secured

name: Helmand River Valley Offensive
description: 2008. The Marines came to Helmand. Not to join the British, who'd been fighting there since 2006 — but to surge. Helmand province produced 60% of Afghanistan's opium, which meant 60% of the world's heroin supply, which meant the Taliban had more money than they could spend. The 2nd Marine Expeditionary Brigade pushed into the Helmand River valley — into Now Zad, Garmsir, Khan Neshin — places where the British had been pinned down or couldn't reach. The Marines brought a different philosophy: overwhelm with force, push out into the population, and hold. But Helmand was vast, the enemy was deep, and the IED threat was unlike anything the Marines had faced. Every foot patrol was a walk through a minefield — literal, not figurative. The sappers went first with metal detectors, moving at a pace that made you feel naked and exposed for hours. One step at a time. The Helmand River valley in 2008 was the opening chapter of a surge that would define the war's final decade, and every word of it was written at walking speed through fields that wanted to kill you.
belligerents: US Marines / British Forces / Afghan Army
result: Expanded coalition presence — long-term control remained elusive

name: Battle of Now Zad
description: Now Zad was a ghost town. The British had fought there in 2006, and the fighting was so intense that the entire civilian population left. By 2008, it was a bizarre artifact: an empty city occupied by Taliban fighters on one side and a British/Estonian garrison on the other, separated by a few hundred meters of no-man's-land that looked like something from the Western Front. The Marines of 2/7 came in December 2008 and did what the constrained British force couldn't: they swept through the city, cleared the Taliban positions, and opened the road. The fighting was sharp but brief — the Taliban chose to fade rather than stand against a full Marine battalion. But Now Zad's story isn't really about the battle. It's about what happens when a town dies because of war. The empty houses, the abandoned bazaar, the silence where a community used to be. When the Marines walked those streets, they were walking through a monument to what war costs — not in casualties, but in the ordinary life it erases.
result: Marines clear city — civilians slowly return

name: Battle of Garmsir
description: Garmsir, southern Helmand. The 24th Marine Expeditionary Unit pushed deep into territory the Taliban had held for two years. The frontline — and there was an actual, traceable frontline, like a 20th-century war — ran through irrigated farmland along the Helmand River. The Marines advanced through compounds and tree lines, clearing IEDs as they went, fighting through ambushes that erupted from positions invisible until the first RPG streaked past. The British had held a line at Garmsir for months, unable to advance with the forces they had. The Marines broke through in weeks, pushing the Taliban south toward the Pakistan border. But the true cost of Garmsir was measured in the IED strikes on the routes behind the advancing companies — the supply lines that had to stay open through terrain the Taliban salted with explosives like a farmer sowing seeds. Garmsir was won by Marines moving forward and engineers keeping the roads open behind them, and both jobs could kill you any given day.
result: Coalition advance — Taliban pushed south

name: Qala-i-Naw Operations (Badghis Province)
description: Badghis province. North of Herat, south of Turkmenistan, and firmly within the western Afghanistan area of operations that ran through Shindand. The Spanish PRT at Qala-i-Naw was the coalition's footprint in a province where the Taliban controlled the countryside and the government controlled the capital — barely. Spanish forces, with US support, ran clearing operations through districts that saw fighting spike during the 2010-2012 period as the Taliban pushed into areas previously considered stable. Badghis wasn't Helmand — the fighting was lower intensity but no less dangerous for the soldiers on patrol. IEDs, sniper fire, ambushes on convoys. The Spanish took casualties, and so did the Afghans. For Americans supporting operations from Shindand and forward bases, Badghis was part of the daily reality of western Afghanistan: a province that was too unstable to ignore and too vast to control.
belligerents: Spanish ISAF / US Forces / Afghan Army
result: Sustained security operations — gradual transition to Afghan lead

name: Operation Rhino (Objective Rhino)
description: October 19, 2001. The first boots on the ground in the Afghan war. Two hundred Rangers from the 75th Ranger Regiment parachuted onto a desert airstrip south of Kandahar — Objective Rhino — in a combat jump that was equal parts military operation and message to the world: we're here. Simultaneously, Delta Force operators hit one of Mullah Omar's compounds near Kandahar. The airstrip seizure was a raid, not an occupation — the Rangers were in and out in hours, taking fire from Taliban positions and collecting intelligence. It was the opening act of a war that would last twenty years, and the Rangers who jumped that night had no idea they were starting something that wouldn't end until they were middle-aged. The jump footage — grainy green night-vision video of paratroopers floating into a black Afghan desert — became iconic. What it didn't show was the fear, the chaos, the twisted ankles on rocky ground, and the tracers that came from directions the briefing didn't mention. The war's first hours set the tone for everything that followed: bold, violent, and not quite what anyone expected.
result: Raid successful — intelligence gathered, message sent

name: Battle of Mazar-i-Sharif
description: November 2001. The battle that proved the unconventional warfare model could topple a regime. CIA paramilitary officers and Special Forces ODAs rode into Mazar-i-Sharif on horseback — literally on horseback — alongside Northern Alliance cavalry, calling in B-52 strikes on Taliban positions while galloping across the Dari-a-Souf Valley. The image of American Special Forces soldiers on horseback, using lasers to guide 21st-century bombs onto a medieval battlefield, became the defining visual of the early Afghan war. The Horse Soldiers. The Taliban defensive lines collapsed under the combination of precision air power and Afghan cavalry charges. Mazar-i-Sharif fell on November 9, 2001 — less than a month after the air campaign began. It was intoxicating. Quick, decisive, minimal American casualties. The model that every general and politician pointed to as proof that wars could be won cheap and fast. They were wrong. But on that day, in that valley, on those horses — it looked like the future of warfare.
belligerents: US Special Forces / CIA / Northern Alliance
result: Northern Alliance / US victory — first major city liberated
commanders: ODA 595 / CIA SAD / Gen Abdul Rashid Dostum

name: Battle of Qala-i-Jangi
description: November 25, 2001. A 19th-century fortress near Mazar-i-Sharif. Hundreds of Taliban and al-Qaeda prisoners who'd supposedly surrendered were being held in the courtyard when they rushed their guards, seized weapons from an armory, and turned Qala-i-Jangi into a three-day battle that killed CIA officer Johnny 'Mike' Spann — the first American combat death of the Afghan war. Spann had been interrogating prisoners minutes before the uprising. His partner, David Tyson, fought his way out with a pistol and an AK taken from a dead fighter. Special Forces teams, British SBS operators, and Northern Alliance soldiers fought to retake the fortress compound by compound. They called in airstrikes on their own position. One JDAM hit a Northern Alliance command post, wounding five Americans. The prisoners who survived — including John Walker Lindh, the 'American Taliban' — were discovered in a flooded basement after the fortress was retaken. Qala-i-Jangi was the first sign that the Afghan war would be more brutal, more chaotic, and more morally complex than anyone imagined.
belligerents: US Special Forces / CIA / British SBS / Northern Alliance
result: Uprising suppressed — first US combat death (CIA officer Mike Spann)
commanders: CIA officers / US SOF / Gen Dostum's forces

name: Battle of Debecka Pass
description: April 6, 2003. Debecka Pass, northern Iraq. Halfway between Kirkuk and Erbil. An ODA of Green Berets and a company of Kurdish Peshmerga ran head-on into an Iraqi mechanized counterattack — tanks, APCs, and hundreds of troops rolling straight at them. Twenty-six Americans and maybe a hundred Kurds against an armored force. The Green Berets had Javelin missiles. What happened next became a Special Forces legend: the Javelins tore through the Iraqi armor at ranges that stunned the Iraqis. Vehicles brewed up. The counterattack stalled. But then Iraqi artillery started landing, and the Peshmerga began to waver. The SF team held their ground and kept calling in air strikes until the Iraqis broke and retreated. This was the northern front in miniature: a handful of Americans with precision weapons and iron nerve, holding a line that should have been held by a division. Debecka Pass is why the Green Berets who served in northern Iraq walk differently. They know what they did with almost nothing.
belligerents: US Special Forces ODA / Kurdish Peshmerga
result: US/Kurdish victory — Iraqi counterattack destroyed

name: Battle of the Karbala Gap
description: Early April 2003. The Karbala Gap — a narrow corridor between Karbala and the Euphrates — was supposed to be where the Republican Guard made its stand. The Medina Division had dug in along this chokepoint, the last natural defensive position before Baghdad. Intelligence expected chemical weapons here. Some units had MOPP suits on. The 3rd Infantry Division punched through at night, in a sandstorm, with artillery and air support raining on the Medina Division positions. The Iraqis fought — this wasn't the walkover that some units experienced elsewhere — but they were outmatched by an American force that could see in the dark and shoot in the weather. The Medina Division was effectively destroyed. The road to Baghdad was open. For the tankers and Bradley crews who drove through the Karbala Gap at speed, this was the moment the invasion pivoted from 'fight your way through' to 'race to the capital.' The chemical weapons never materialized. The fear of them was real enough.
result: US breakthrough — Medina Division destroyed, road to Baghdad opened

name: Battle of Najaf
description: March-April 2003. Najaf — one of the holiest cities in Shia Islam, home to the Imam Ali Mosque. The 101st Airborne fought through and around Najaf against Fedayeen Saddam fighters who used the city's dense urban terrain and religious significance as a shield. You couldn't bomb near the mosque. You couldn't drive tanks through the narrow streets without risk of hitting a shrine that would turn every Shia Muslim in the world against you. The Fedayeen knew this. They fought from positions that dared the Americans to cause collateral damage to sites sacred to 200 million people. The 101st had to be surgeons with a sledgehammer. They cleared Najaf methodically, painfully, taking casualties in fights where the rules of engagement were as much a factor as the enemy. Najaf would explode again in 2004 when Muqtada al-Sadr's Mahdi Army occupied the city and the Imam Ali Mosque itself — but in 2003, it was the first lesson in a war where the terrain was as much political as physical.
result: US victory — city secured, holy sites preserved

name: Battle of Baqubah (Operation Arrowhead Ripper)
description: June 2007. Baqubah, capital of Diyala province. Al-Qaeda in Iraq had declared it the capital of their Islamic State — before ISIS made that rebranding official. The 3rd Stryker Brigade and 5th Battalion, 20th Infantry went in during the surge, supported by Apache helicopters and air strikes. AQI had prepared: they'd rigged entire neighborhoods with deep-buried IEDs, created fighting positions in houses, and mined the roads with everything from artillery shells to propane tanks packed with ball bearings. The first day, Strykers hit massive IEDs that blew vehicles into the air. The infantry went house to house through a city where every doorway could be booby-trapped and every rooftop could hide a sniper. The operation took weeks. When it was over, massive weapons caches were found — enough to fight for months. Baqubah was the surge in microcosm: go where the enemy is strongest, take the hit, and hold the ground until the population believes you're not leaving.
belligerents: US 3rd Stryker Brigade / 5-20 Infantry / Iraqi Army
result: Coalition victory — AQI leadership destroyed or displaced

name: Battle of Haditha Dam
description: April 1, 2003. Army Rangers and Special Operations forces seized Haditha Dam on the Euphrates in a lightning operation that prevented one of the coalition's worst nightmares: Saddam's forces blowing the dam and flooding the valley downstream, potentially killing thousands of civilians and cutting the invasion's supply lines. The Rangers held the dam for five days against Iraqi counterattacks while the main invasion force advanced north. They were isolated, outnumbered, and running low on everything. Air resupply was sporadic. The dam itself was wired with explosives that the Rangers had to clear while defending it. For five days, a Ranger battalion held critical infrastructure against an enemy that knew losing the dam meant losing the war. It was one of those operations that only gets its due recognition years later — when you read the details and realize how close things came to going catastrophically wrong. Haditha Dam held. The invasion continued. The Rangers who held it know what it cost.
result: Dam secured — catastrophic flooding prevented

name: Abu Ghraib Area Operations
description: Abu Ghraib. The name conjures the photos — the scandal that did more damage to America's standing in the Muslim world than any military defeat. But Abu Ghraib was also a military district west of Baghdad where some of the war's hardest fighting took place. The roads through Abu Ghraib to Fallujah were some of the most IED'd in Iraq. Convoys running Route Irish and the western highways took contact so regularly that the soldiers driving them developed a sixth sense for disturbed earth and suspicious objects — or they didn't, and they paid for it. The Abu Ghraib area was the transition zone between Baghdad and Anbar province, and every bad actor from both sides passed through it. For the units pulling security in that district, the prison scandal turned their mission poisonous — Iraqi civilians who might have cooperated now saw every American as a potential torturer. The tactical cost of that scandal was measured in intelligence that dried up, cooperation that vanished, and soldiers who died because information stopped flowing. Abu Ghraib the place, and Abu Ghraib the catastrophe, are inseparable.
result: Ongoing contested security operations

name: Operation Phantom Thunder (Baghdad Surge)
description: June 2007. The surge wasn't a single battle — it was a fundamental change in how the war was fought. General Petraeus sent 30,000 additional troops into Iraq, and instead of commuting to the fight from big FOBs, they moved into the neighborhoods. Joint Security Stations. Combat Outposts. Living among the people they were trying to protect, in buildings that took mortar fire and car bombs. The idea was simple and terrifying: you can't protect people from behind blast walls. Operation Phantom Thunder was the corps-level offensive that kicked it off — simultaneous operations across the Baghdad belts, Diyala, Anbar, and northern Iraq. Every unit in theater was committed. The casualty rates spiked — May and June 2007 were the bloodiest months of the entire war. But the surge worked. Not perfectly, not permanently, but it worked. Violence dropped. Markets reopened. People came outside. For every soldier and Marine who served the surge, 2007 was the year the war felt like it might actually have a point. That feeling didn't last. But for a moment, it was real.
belligerents: US Surge Forces / Iraqi Security Forces
result: Significant violence reduction — conditions for political progress
commanders: GEN David Petraeus / LTG Raymond Odierno

name: Siege of Kobani
description: September 2014 to January 2015. Kobani, on the Syrian-Turkish border. ISIS threw everything they had at this Kurdish city — tanks, artillery, suicide bombers, thousands of fighters. The world watched on live television as Turkish citizens stood on hilltops across the border and watched the city burn. The Kurdish YPG/YPJ fighters — including female fighters who terrified ISIS because they believed being killed by a woman denied them paradise — held block by block with small arms and courage that defied logic. US airstrikes, eventually authorized after weeks of hand-wringing, turned the tide. Over 700 airstrikes in four months, the most concentrated air campaign since the invasion of Iraq. ISIS lost an estimated 2,000 fighters at Kobani — their first major strategic defeat. The battle proved that ISIS could be beaten, and it forged the US-Kurdish partnership that would eventually retake Raqqa. For every veteran of northern Iraq who'd worked alongside Kurds, Kobani hit close to home. Those were the same people — Kurds fighting for their existence, again, with minimal support, again.
result: Kurdish / Coalition victory — ISIS first major strategic defeat

name: Battle of Raqqa
description: June to October 2017. The 'capital' of the ISIS caliphate. The city where they filmed beheadings, where they planned the Paris attacks, where they built their slave markets. The Syrian Democratic Forces — primarily Kurdish YPG fighters with embedded US Special Operations advisors — went in to end it. Four months of house-to-house combat through a city that ISIS had spent three years fortifying. Tunnels under every neighborhood. IEDs in every building. Snipers in minarets. Suicide bombers on every street corner. The SDF lost over 600 fighters. Civilian casualties were devastating — the UN estimated that coalition airstrikes killed hundreds. The city was gutted. When the last ISIS fighters surrendered in the stadium in October, Raqqa looked like Dresden. The caliphate's capital was rubble. For anyone who'd watched ISIS metastasize from Iraq to Syria and back, who'd watched them take Mosul and Kirkuk and Raqqa, the fall of their so-called capital felt like a turning point. The caliphate was dead. But the ideology that built it was just as alive as the day they raised their black flag.
belligerents: Syrian Democratic Forces / US Special Operations / Coalition air
result: SDF / Coalition victory — ISIS 'capital' destroyed

name: Battle of Baiji Refinery
description: 2014-2015. The Baiji oil refinery — Iraq's largest — sat on the road between Baghdad and Kirkuk, and ISIS wanted it. The siege lasted over a year. Iraqi security forces held the refinery complex while ISIS controlled the surrounding town, turning every resupply attempt into a running battle. Convoys pushing north from Tikrit had to fight through ISIS checkpoints, IEDs, and ambushes just to deliver food and ammunition to the garrison. Iraqi soldiers held that refinery through heat, hunger, and constant attack because losing Baiji meant losing Iraq's oil infrastructure and the highway north to Kirkuk. When the complex was finally secured in late 2015, the refinery was destroyed — damaged beyond repair by the fighting. Iraq 'won' Baiji but lost the refinery that was the reason for fighting there. For northern Iraq veterans, Baiji was on the road you drove. The refinery was the landmark you navigated by. Watching it burn on satellite imagery felt like watching your own history go up in smoke.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Shia militia / Coalition air
result: Iraqi forces hold — refinery destroyed in fighting

name: Liberation of Tikrit from ISIS
description: March 2015. Tikrit again. The same city the 4th Infantry Division took in 2003. Now it was Iraqi forces — regular army, Shia militias, and eventually US air support — retaking it from ISIS. The operation was controversial from the start: Iranian-backed Shia militias, including some the US had fought against in Baghdad, were the primary ground force. Qassem Soleimani was photographed on the front lines, directing operations. The city was rigged with IEDs — ISIS had turned Tikrit into a bomb factory, and every building was potentially wired. The coalition initially refused to provide air support because of the militia presence. When the ground assault stalled, Iraq had to choose: Iranian support or American support. They chose American air power, the militias pulled back, and the Iraqi army pushed through. Tikrit was liberated, but the political dynamics of who fought for it and who controlled it afterward foreshadowed the fractured, militia-dominated Iraq that would emerge from the ISIS war.
belligerents: Iraqi Army / Shia PMF militias / Coalition air
result: Iraqi victory — Tikrit liberated from ISIS
commanders: Iraqi Joint Operations Command / Shia militia leaders

name: Sinjar Massacre and Siege
description: August 2014. ISIS came to Sinjar, and they came to exterminate. The Yazidis — a religious minority that ISIS declared 'devil worshippers' — had nowhere to run. The Peshmerga who were supposed to protect them withdrew without fighting. Thousands of Yazidis fled to Mount Sinjar in temperatures exceeding 120 degrees. On the plains below, ISIS was executing men by the hundreds and enslaving women and girls. This wasn't war — this was genocide. The UN would later formally recognize it as such. US airstrikes and airdrops kept the people on the mountain alive, and a corridor was eventually opened for them to escape through Syria. But what ISIS did in Sinjar — the mass graves, the slave markets, the systematic destruction of a people — was the act that changed the war. It was the reason the US came back to Iraq. Not for oil, not for politics, but because what was happening was an atrocity that demanded a response. For anyone who'd served in northern Iraq, who'd worked with Yazidi interpreters, who'd driven through Sinjar district, the images from August 2014 were personal in a way that transcended professional military concern. Those were people you knew.
belligerents: Yazidi civilians / Kurdish YPG/PKK / US air support
result: Genocide of Yazidis — international intervention prevents total massacre

name: Operation Red Dawn (Capture of Saddam)
description: December 13, 2003. Ad-Dawr, a village south of Tikrit. Operation Red Dawn. The 4th Infantry Division and Special Operations forces had been hunting Saddam Hussein for eight months, working through his network of enablers, bodyguards, and tribal connections. The intelligence chain that led to the spider hole was built on months of patient, grueling human intelligence work — interrogations, tips, surveillance, and the slow unraveling of a fugitive network. When they pulled him out of a hole barely large enough for a man to lie down in, dirty, bearded, and disoriented, the 'Ace of Spades' was off the deck. The man who'd terrorized thirty million people was found hiding like a rat. For every soldier who'd served in northern Iraq — who'd driven past the palaces, who'd searched the compounds, who'd heard the rumors that he was still nearby — Saddam's capture was a strange kind of closure. The dictator was caught. The war he started was nowhere near over. But for one night, in one hole, it felt like something had been finished.
result: Saddam Hussein captured alive
commanders: MG Raymond Odierno / SOF task force

name: Battle of Musa Qala
description: December 2007. Musa Qala, northern Helmand. The only Afghan district center the Taliban had captured and held — an embarrassment for ISAF and the Afghan government. The British had cut a controversial deal with tribal elders in 2006 to let them handle security. The Taliban walked right through the elders and took over. Retaking it became a matter of prestige. Operation Mar Karadad — 'Snake Pit' — was an Afghan-led operation, or at least that's what the press releases said. In reality, US Special Forces, British commandos, and massive coalition air support did the heavy lifting while the Afghan National Army provided the flag. The paratroopers air-assaulted into blocking positions around the town while ground forces pushed in. The Taliban fought hard, then melted into the desert as they always did. Musa Qala was 'liberated,' but the pattern — take, hold, transfer, lose, retake — was becoming depressingly familiar to anyone paying attention. The town changed hands like a chess piece, and the soldiers who fought for it each time were left wondering when the cycle would end.
belligerents: Afghan National Army / US SOF / British Forces / ISAF
result: Coalition / Afghan victory — district center retaken

name: Lashkar Gah Operations
description: Lashkar Gah. The capital of Helmand province. The 'model city' where the British concentrated their development efforts and the Americans poured resources. It was supposed to be the proof of concept — if counterinsurgency could work anywhere, it would work in the provincial capital with the most resources per capita of anywhere in Afghanistan. The Taliban responded by infiltrating bomb makers into the suburbs and turning the approaches to the city into IED belts. Operations around Lashkar Gah from 2010 onward were a constant push and pull: clear the outskirts, establish checkpoints, build roads, hand off to Afghan police — and watch the progress erode when the unit rotated out and the replacements learned the same lessons all over again. The Afghan police in Lashkar Gah ranged from competent to predatory. The politics were Byzantine. And in August 2021, Lashkar Gah fell to the Taliban in days. The model city, the proof of concept, the billions invested — gone. The lesson of Lashkar Gah is the lesson of the entire war compressed into a single city.
belligerents: British Forces / US Forces / Afghan Security Forces
result: Sustained operations — city fell to Taliban in 2021
commanders: British Task Force Helmand / US Marines

name: Adraskan District Operations (Herat)
description: Adraskan district, southeast of Shindand in Herat province. Close enough to Shindand airbase that you could hear the jets taking off, remote enough that the government's presence was a flag on a building and not much else. The terrain around Adraskan — dry riverbeds, scattered compounds, open desert punctuated by irrigated valleys — was classic Afghan ambush country.
