Coalition and Afghan forces ran periodic clearing operations through the district, responding to intelligence about weapons caches, IED factories, or Taliban commanders transiting the area. Every operation followed the same rhythm: helicopter insertion or ground movement at dawn, cordon and search through the morning, contact in the afternoon when the fighters decided they'd observed long enough, extraction before dark. Adraskan wasn't on anyone's war map back home. But for the people who operated from Shindand, Adraskan was the backyard — close enough to reach, dangerous enough to respect, and representative of the quiet, grinding war that defined western Afghanistan.
belligerents: US Forces / Afghan National Army / ISAF
result: Periodic clearing operations — area remained contested

name: Nimroz Province Border Operations
description: Nimroz province. The southwestern corner of Afghanistan, bordering both Iran and Pakistan, making it a smuggler's paradise and a nightmare for border security. Drugs going out, weapons coming in, fighters crossing in both directions. The Afghan Border Police in Nimroz were among the most corrupt in the country — which is saying something. Operations from Shindand and other western bases regularly extended into Nimroz to interdict drug shipments and disrupt Taliban logistics. The Iranian border added a dimension that other theaters didn't have: Iranian intelligence services were active in western Afghanistan, playing all sides, supplying weapons to the Taliban while officially cooperating with the Afghan government. For US forces in RC-West, the Iranian angle was the subtext of everything. Every captured EFP, every intercepted shipment, every informant's report about 'visitors from the west' pointed toward Tehran. Nimroz was where the Afghan war, the drug war, and the shadow war with Iran all overlapped.
belligerents: US Forces / ISAF / Afghan Border Police
result: Ongoing interdiction operations — smuggling routes never fully disrupted

name: Battle of Uzbin Valley (French Ambush)
description: August 18, 2008. Uzbin Valley, Sarobi district, east of Kabul. A French patrol of about 100 soldiers walked into a Taliban ambush that killed ten of them and wounded twenty-one. It was the worst single-day loss for ISAF outside of the US and UK, and it shocked France to its core. The French soldiers fought with extraordinary bravery — hand-to-hand combat, ammunition running out, medevac helicopters unable to land due to the terrain and fire. The ambush was meticulously planned: the Taliban had let previous patrols through the same route unmolested, building a false sense of security. Then they sealed both ends of the valley and opened fire from the ridgelines. For coalition forces everywhere — including those in western Afghanistan working alongside European allies — Uzbin was a reminder that the Taliban were patient, adaptive, and capable of inflicting devastating losses on any force that got complacent. The French response was to escalate their commitment, but Uzbin changed how France thought about the war permanently.
result: French tactical defeat — 10 KIA, 21 wounded

name: Kunduz — MSF Hospital Airstrike
description: October 3, 2015. A US AC-130 gunship circled over Kunduz and systematically destroyed a Medecins Sans Frontieres trauma hospital for over an hour. Forty-two people killed — patients, doctors, nurses. The crew received coordinates that they were told was a Taliban command post. The hospital had shared its GPS coordinates with all parties to the conflict. The strike continued even after MSF frantically called US and Afghan officials reporting they were being bombed. The investigation found a cascade of human error and system failure, but 'error' doesn't cover it for the families of the dead. Kunduz was the moment that tested what America says about itself — about accountability, about the laws of war, about the value of human life. No criminal charges were filed. For anyone who served in Afghanistan, who called in air support, who trusted the system that was supposed to prevent exactly this — Kunduz is a wound. Because every one of us knows we were one bad coordinate, one miscommunication, one exhausted JTAC away from our own Kunduz.
belligerents: US Air Force AC-130 / Afghan Ground Forces
result: Hospital destroyed — 42 killed, international condemnation
commanders: N/A — hospital was the target in error

name: Arghandab Valley Operations
description: The Arghandab River Valley, north of Kandahar city. Orchards, pomegranates, grape rows, irrigation canals — lush, green, and saturated with IEDs. The 101st Airborne and later the 82nd Airborne fought here in some of the most intense close combat of the surge. The Taliban had seeded the valley so thoroughly with pressure-plate IEDs that companies were losing soldiers weekly to devices that couldn't be detected by electronic means — no metal, no wires, just a jug of homemade explosive and a wooden plate. Soldiers resorted to walking in single file, stepping exactly in the footprints of the man ahead. When a path was cleared, the Taliban would mine it again overnight. The pomegranate orchards provided perfect concealment for ambushes, and the mud-brick compound walls were thick enough to stop anything short of a tank round. Arghandab was the war at its most primal — men on foot, in orchards, stepping carefully, knowing that every step could be their last. No technology could save you. Just your eyes, your training, and whatever luck you had left.
result: Contested clearing operations — IED threat never fully eliminated

name: Wardak Province Operations
description: Wardak province. The gateway to Kabul from the south and west. Highway 1 — the ring road connecting Kabul to Kandahar — ran through Wardak, making it the Taliban's preferred route for moving fighters and weapons toward the capital. Securing Wardak meant securing Kabul, and the Taliban knew it. The 10th Mountain Division, the 82nd Airborne, and eventually Afghan commandos fought a relentless campaign through the valleys and mountain passes of Wardak against an enemy that could melt across the provincial boundary into Logar or Ghazni when the pressure got too hot. The Tangi Valley in Wardak was where a Chinook carrying 30 Americans — including 17 Navy SEALs from DEVGRU — was shot down in August 2011. Extortion 17. The single deadliest incident of the entire war. For every American who served in Afghanistan, that call sign is a scar. Thirty brothers. One RPG. One valley that nobody back home had ever heard of.
result: Sustained contested operations — area never fully secured

name: Extortion 17 Shootdown
description: August 6, 2011. Tangi Valley, Wardak province. A CH-47D Chinook — call sign Extortion 17 — was shot down by an RPG while inserting a quick reaction force to support Army Rangers in contact. Thirty Americans died, including seventeen members of Naval Special Warfare Development Group — the same unit that had killed Osama bin Laden three months earlier. Eight Afghan commandos and one interpreter also died. It was the deadliest single event of the entire twenty-year war. The helicopter was on approach to a landing zone in a valley where the enemy had the high ground — the oldest problem in mountain warfare, and the most lethal. One Taliban fighter with an RPG-7, a weapon designed in the 1960s, brought down a helicopter carrying some of the most elite operators in the world. The investigation found no systemic failure, just the brutal mathematics of war: put enough helicopters into enough valleys, and eventually the enemy gets lucky. For everyone who served in 2011, Extortion 17 was the day the war reached up and took the best we had.
result: 30 US and 8 Afghan personnel killed — deadliest single event of the war

name: Operation Neptune Spear (Bin Laden Raid)
description: May 2, 2011. Abbottabad, Pakistan. DEVGRU operators in stealth-modified Black Hawks cross the Pakistani border at night and hit a compound 800 meters from Pakistan's military academy. Twenty-three SEALs, a translator, and a dog named Cairo assault the compound in a mission planned over months and executed in thirty-eight minutes. One Black Hawk crashes in the courtyard — the mission almost dies right there — but the operators adapt, breach, and clear the building floor by floor. On the third floor, they find him. Osama bin Laden. The man who murdered 2,977 people on September 11, 2001. Two rounds. It's done. For everyone who served in Afghanistan and Iraq — for every person who deployed because of what happened on that Tuesday morning — the call 'Geronimo EKIA' closed a chapter. Not the war. The chapter. Ten years, thousands of lives, trillions of dollars, and it came down to two rounds in a bedroom in Pakistan. The war went on for another decade. But for one night, it felt like something had been answered.
result: Bin Laden killed — most wanted terrorist eliminated

name: Operation Medusa (Panjwai)
description: September 2006. The Canadian military's hardest fight since Korea. Operation Medusa in Panjwai district, Kandahar province. The Taliban had massed over a thousand fighters — conventional force, not guerrillas — in the green zone west of Kandahar city, daring NATO to come get them. The Canadians of Task Force 3-06 went in with LAVs, artillery, and close air support. The fighting was face-to-face in the grape rows and irrigation ditches. Taliban fighters were dug in behind mud walls that could stop a 25mm round. Canadian soldiers were fighting in temperatures above 50 degrees Celsius, loaded with 80 pounds of kit, in terrain designed by nature to conceal the defender. Twelve Canadians killed. Hundreds of Taliban. Medusa was a tactical victory that exposed a strategic problem: the Taliban could regenerate faster than NATO could kill them. The fighters who died in Panjwai were replaced within months by new recruits from the madrassas of Pakistan. But for the Canadians — and for every coalition partner watching — Medusa proved that NATO would fight. The alliance was tested in the dirt of Panjwai, and it held.
result: NATO / Afghan victory — Taliban conventional force defeated

name: Kunar Province Operations
description: Kunar province. The border province that never stopped bleeding. Wedged between Nuristan and Pakistan, the valleys of Kunar — Pech, Korengal, Watapur, Shuryak — were the Afghan war's permanent contact zones. The terrain was vertical. The enemy had unlimited safe haven across the Pakistani border. And the people of Kunar were caught between a Taliban they feared and a government they didn't trust and Americans they couldn't understand. Operations in Kunar were a revolving door: clear a valley, establish an outpost, take contact every day, rotate out, let the next unit learn the same lessons. The Pech River Valley alone consumed multiple battalions over the course of the war. Kunar was where the Afghan war went to prove its critics right — you couldn't seal the border, you couldn't win the population in valleys where the government had never provided anything, and you couldn't kill your way to stability in terrain that regenerated fighters like a forest regenerates trees.
result: Permanent contested zone — most outposts eventually abandoned

name: Battle of Ganjgal
description: September 8, 2009. Ganjgal, Kunar province. A patrol of US Marines, Army advisors, and Afghan soldiers walked into a village for a shura and walked into a kill zone. The ambush was devastating — fire from three sides, pre-registered mortar positions, and an enemy that had every approach dialed in. When the Americans called for artillery support, the fire missions were denied four times. Four times. Bureaucratic caution about civilian casualties — the legacy of incidents like Azizabad — meant that soldiers under fire couldn't get the support they needed to survive. Marine Corporal Dakota Meyer drove back into the kill zone five times in a humvee to pull out wounded and dead, earning the Medal of Honor. Army Captain Will Swenson did the same, also earning the Medal of Honor — the only battle in Afghanistan to produce two living Medal of Honor recipients. Five Americans and eight Afghans died because the system designed to protect civilians inadvertently sacrificed the men it was supposed to support. Ganjgal is the battle that makes you understand why soldiers come home angry.
belligerents: US Marines / Army ETT / Afghan Army
result: US/Afghan forces survive ambush — 5 US KIA, 8 Afghan KIA
commanders: CPT Will Swenson / CPL Dakota Meyer

name: Battle of Takur Ghar (Roberts Ridge)
description: March 4, 2002. A Chinook inserting a SEAL reconnaissance team onto a 10,000-foot mountain peak took RPG fire. Navy SEAL Neil Roberts fell from the helicopter into an al-Qaeda position and fought alone until he was killed. The rest of the team went back for him. A second helicopter carrying Rangers was sent to rescue the rescuers and was shot down on the same peak. What followed was seventeen hours of close combat at altitude, in the snow, against a fortified al-Qaeda position that had every advantage terrain could offer. Seven Americans killed. The fight for Roberts Ridge became legendary — a story of loyalty so fierce that men flew into a kill zone they knew was a kill zone because leaving a brother behind wasn't something they could do. The tactical failure is documented in every after-action review: multiple helicopters sent to the same hot LZ, piecemeal reinforcement, communication breakdown between SOF elements. The human story transcends the tactical failure. They went back. They always went back.
belligerents: US Navy SEALs / 75th Rangers / USAF Combat Controllers
result: Peak secured — 7 US KIA in desperate close combat
commanders: SEAL team leader / Ranger QRF commander

name: Operation Red Wings
description: June 28, 2005. Kunar province. A four-man SEAL reconnaissance team — Marcus Luttrell, Michael Murphy, Danny Dietz, and Matthew Axelson — was compromised by goatherds on a mountain above the Shuryek Valley. The team released the goatherds rather than kill civilians. Within hours, 30-50 Taliban fighters were on them. The team fought downhill through impossible terrain, outnumbered and cut off. Michael Murphy, already wounded, walked into the open to get satellite phone signal and called for help, earning a posthumous Medal of Honor. The QRF helicopter — a Chinook with eight SEALs and eight Night Stalkers — was shot down by an RPG. Everyone aboard killed. Total: nineteen Americans dead. Marcus Luttrell survived alone, rescued by Pashtun villagers who invoked Pashtunwali — the code of hospitality and protection. 'Lone Survivor' made it famous. What the book doesn't fully convey is the scope of the loss — sixteen special operators and three aircrew, gone in an afternoon, in a valley most Americans couldn't find with a map and a guide. The deadliest day for Naval Special Warfare since World War II.
result: 19 US KIA — reconnaissance mission catastrophic failure
commanders: LT Michael Murphy (KIA, MOH) / LCDR Erik Kristensen (KIA)

name: Operations in Nahr-e Saraj (Helmand)
description: Nahr-e Saraj — the district between Lashkar Gah and Gereshk in central Helmand. British and later US Marines turned this into the laboratory for population-centric counterinsurgency. Patrol bases every few kilometers. Daily foot patrols. Market engagement. Shuras with elders. And IEDs. God, the IEDs. Nahr-e Saraj was one of the most heavily mined areas in Afghanistan, and the British took a staggering number of amputations here. The compound patterns — walled agricultural plots connected by narrow paths through irrigated fields — created a tactical environment where every step was a decision and every decision could be your last leg. The Marines who took over brought more resources but faced the same problem: the Taliban could mine the ground faster than engineers could clear it. Nahr-e Saraj was where the counterinsurgency theory met the reality of an enemy who didn't need to fight you directly. They just needed to make the ground itself lethal. Walking. That's what it came down to. Soldiers walking through fields, trusting their training, their equipment, and their luck.
result: Gradual security improvement — heavy IED casualties throughout
commanders: British Task Force / US Marine commanders

name: Agadez Air Base Operations (Niger)
description: Agadez, northern Niger. The US built a $110 million drone base here — Air Base 201 — in the middle of the Sahara, and most Americans never knew it existed. The base was the centerpiece of the US intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance architecture in West Africa, flying MQ-9 Reapers over the vast Saharan spaces where jihadi groups trained, moved, and planned. For the airmen and soldiers deployed to Agadez, it was austere duty in the extreme — desert heat, sandstorms, minimal amenities, and a mission that involved staring at screens for hours tracking targets in some of the most remote terrain on earth. The base was controversial with Nigerien politicians and locals who didn't want an American military presence in their backyard. The airmen who served there knew they were in the other war — the one fought with drones and signals intelligence and partner-force training in a region that was slowly being consumed by jihadi insurgencies that nobody back home was paying attention to. Agadez was the future of American warfare: small footprint, low visibility, high stakes, and zero public awareness.
belligerents: ISIS-GS / JNIM / various Saharan jihadi groups
result: Operational ISR hub established — significant intelligence gathering

name: Diffa Region Operations (Niger-Nigeria Border)
description: Diffa, southeastern Niger. The frontline against Boko Haram. The region had been under a state of emergency since 2015, with Boko Haram fighters crossing from northeastern Nigeria to attack villages, markets, and refugee camps. The Nigerien military, supported by US training and intelligence, fought a brutal counterinsurgency against an enemy that specialized in terrorizing civilians — kidnapping, forced recruitment of children, and suicide bombings in crowded markets. US Special Forces trained Nigerien units that deployed to Diffa, and US ISR assets provided the eyes in the sky. For Americans deployed to Niger, Diffa was the reminder that the jihadi threat wasn't just in the Sahara to the north — it was also pressing in from the southeast, a two-front problem for a country with limited resources and unlimited frontage. The human suffering in Diffa — hundreds of thousands of displaced people, entire villages depopulated — was staggering and invisible to the outside world.
result: Ongoing counterinsurgency — civilian displacement massive

name: Menaka Region Operations (Mali-Niger Border)
description: The Menaka region of eastern Mali, right up against the Niger border. This was the operational backyard for anyone deployed to western Niger — the area where ISIS-Greater Sahara recruited, trained, and launched the kind of attacks that killed four Americans at Tongo Tongo. French Barkhane forces and Malian troops operated in the region with US intelligence support, trying to disrupt the jihadi networks that flowed back and forth across borders that existed on maps but not on the ground. The nomadic Tuareg and Fulani populations of the region were caught between competing armed groups — JNIM, ISGS, government militias, and tribal self-defense forces — in a conflict so complex that the 'good guys' and 'bad guys' changed depending on which village you were in. For the Americans operating in this space, Menaka was a lesson in the limits of the partner-force model: you can train and equip and advise, but you can't impose order on a region where the state has never been present and where the population has learned to survive by accommodation rather than resistance.
belligerents: French Barkhane / Malian Army / US ISR support
result: Ongoing contested security — jihadi presence persistent

name: Battle of Gardez
description: January-March 2002. Gardez, Paktia province. Before Anaconda, there was Gardez — a confused, messy battle that foreshadowed every problem the Afghan war would face. CIA teams and Special Forces tried to use Afghan militia proxies to clear al-Qaeda from compounds south of the city. The proxies double-dealt — some were feeding intelligence to al-Qaeda, others were settling personal scores under the cover of American firepower. Friendly fire incidents hit Afghan allies. Tribal politics turned every military decision into a negotiation. And the al-Qaeda fighters in the mountains south of Gardez were harder to dislodge than anyone expected. Gardez was the war's first lesson in the dangers of proxy warfare: when you fight through intermediaries whose interests don't align with yours, you're not really in control. The intelligence failures and coordination problems at Gardez directly shaped the planning for Operation Anaconda — and Anaconda's problems showed that the lessons hadn't been fully learned.
belligerents: US Special Forces / CIA / Afghan militia proxies
result: Partial success — al-Qaeda disrupted but problems foreshadowed Anaconda

name: Baghdad Security Plan (Fardh al-Qanoon)
description: February 2007. The surge arrives in Baghdad. Operation Fardh al-Qanoon — 'Enforcing the Law.' The concept was brutally simple: instead of commuting to the fight from mega-bases on Baghdad's outskirts, US and Iraqi troops would live in the neighborhoods. Joint Security Stations in every district. Combat outposts on every contested street corner. The first weeks were the most dangerous — establishing positions in neighborhoods where Shia death squads and Sunni bombers operated with impunity. The soldiers building those outposts were taking sniper fire and IED attacks while stacking sandbags. But something shifted. When the population saw that the Americans weren't leaving — that they were sleeping in the same streets, shopping at the same markets, exposed to the same threats — cooperation slowly increased. Tips started flowing. Weapons caches were revealed. The sectarian killing that had turned Baghdad's morgues into processing plants began to slow. The surge in Baghdad worked not because of superior firepower but because soldiers did the hardest thing in counterinsurgency: they made themselves vulnerable alongside the people they were trying to protect.
belligerents: US Surge Forces / Iraqi Security Forces
AQI / Shia death squads / Sunni insurgents
result: Significant violence reduction in Baghdad — sectarian killing decreased
commanders: GEN David Petraeus / LTG Raymond Odierno

name: Battle of Kandahar (Operation Hamkari)
description: 2010. Kandahar. The spiritual birthplace of the Taliban. Operation Hamkari — 'cooperation' — was the surge's main effort in the south, and it was unlike any previous operation. Instead of a dramatic D-Day assault, it was a slow squeeze: building security bubble by security bubble around Kandahar city, clearing the surrounding districts, and gradually choking off Taliban access to the population. The 101st Airborne, later the 4th Infantry Division, worked through Arghandab, Zhari, Panjwai, and the Horn of Panjwai — names that would fill casualty reports for months. Every operation was paired with governance engagement: district governors, provincial councils, development projects. The challenge in Kandahar was that the Taliban wasn't just an insurgency — they were the power structure. They were in the police. They were on the provincial council. They were the cousins and brothers of the men we were trying to empower. Kandahar was the war's most honest mirror: you can't defeat an enemy that is the government and the opposition simultaneously.
result: Temporary security improvement — Taliban influence never fully removed
commanders: MG James Terry / various brigade commanders

name: Battle of Al-Qaim (Operation Steel Curtain)
description: November 2005. Al-Qaim, on the Syrian border. Operation Steel Curtain. The Marines and Iraqi forces went into a string of towns along the Euphrates that had become AQI's ratline from Syria — the pipeline through which foreign fighters, money, and weapons flowed into Iraq. Husaybah, Karabilah, Ubaydi — each one a fortified position that had to be taken house by house. The Marines of the 2nd Marine Division and the Iraqi 7th Division cleared the towns in weeks of close combat against an enemy that knew the terrain and had prepared defensive positions in depth. The border region of western Anbar was the war's back door — everything that made the insurgency lethal passed through these towns. Sealing the Syrian border was an impossible task, but the Marines at Al-Qaim came closer than anyone else. The model they built — combined US-Iraqi operations with permanent security stations — became the template for the Anbar Awakening that followed. The guys who fought at Al-Qaim didn't just clear a town; they proved a concept that changed the war.
result: Coalition victory — border ratline disrupted

name: Camp Taji / Taji Area Operations
description: Camp Taji, north of Baghdad. One of the largest US bases in Iraq, and the surrounding area was a battleground for the entire war. The highways north from Taji toward Samarra and Tikrit — routes that connected Baghdad to northern Iraq, routes that anyone headed to Kirkuk drove — were among the most IED'd roads in the country. The farmland and palm groves around Taji provided concealment for mortar teams that lobbed rounds at the base with depressing regularity. Mahdi Army and Sunni insurgent groups both operated in the area, sometimes fighting each other, sometimes fighting the Americans, sometimes both at once. For anyone transiting between Baghdad and the north — to Kirkuk, to Tikrit, to Mosul — Taji was the first checkpoint, the place where you tightened your kit and started paying attention. The roads north of Taji were where the war started getting personal, because every mile closer to your AO was a mile deeper into territory that wanted you dead.
result: Sustained security operations — routes never fully secured

name: 3rd SFG Sahel Deployment
description: 2017-2018. The 3rd Special Forces Group's deployment to the Sahel — the one that included the Tongo Tongo patrol — was the tip of a much larger American presence in West Africa that most people never knew about. ODAs spread across Niger, Cameroon, Chad, and other nations, training partner forces, building relationships with local military leaders, and conducting patrols in some of the most ungoverned space on earth. These weren't the door-kicking raids of Iraq and Afghanistan. This was the slow, patient work of unconventional warfare: learning languages, understanding tribal dynamics, earning trust in communities where the US military was an alien concept. The Green Berets lived in austere conditions, often in tents or local compounds, with minimal support and long drives through terrain where a breakdown could leave you stranded for days. After Tongo Tongo, the mission changed — more force protection, more restrictions, more oversight. The freewheeling partner-force model that had defined the Sahel mission was reined in. But the mission didn't stop. The quiet war in West Africa continued, invisible to all but the families of the people fighting it.
result: Partner forces trained — mission parameters tightened after Tongo Tongo

name: FOB Chapman Attack (Khost Province)
description: December 30, 2009. Forward Operating Base Chapman, Khost province. A Jordanian double agent named Humam al-Balawi — who the CIA believed was their best source on Ayman al-Zawahiri — walked onto the base, past security, into a meeting with CIA officers and contractors, and detonated his suicide vest. Seven CIA officers killed, including the base chief Jennifer Matthews, along with a Jordanian intelligence officer. It was the deadliest attack on the CIA in twenty-five years. The agency had been so eager for intelligence on al-Qaeda's senior leadership that they'd bypassed standard security protocols — no search, no pat-down — for an asset they believed was too valuable to offend. Al-Balawi had been turned by al-Qaeda and walked right through the door the CIA held open for him. For everyone who served in Afghanistan's border provinces, Chapman was a gut punch. The intelligence community that was supposed to be the brain of the war machine had been deceived at the most fundamental level. If the professionals at Chapman could be fooled, what chance did anyone have of knowing who was who in this war?
result: 7 CIA officers killed — worst CIA loss since 1983 Beirut bombing

name: Battle of Khasham (US vs Russian Mercenaries)
description: February 7, 2018. Deir ez-Zor province, Syria. A force of 500 pro-Assad fighters, including Russian Wagner Group mercenaries, attacked a position held by US Special Operations forces and their SDF allies near Khasham. What happened next was a one-sided annihilation. US forces called in every asset available: F-15Es, B-52s, AC-130 gunships, Apache helicopters, and HIMARS rocket artillery. Over four hours, the attacking force was systematically destroyed. Estimates range from 100 to 300 killed, including a significant number of Russian mercenaries. Moscow denied their presence. The Wagner Group families who never got their sons back know better. Khasham was a moment where the shadow war between the US and Russia in Syria briefly became a shooting war — and the US won so decisively that Russia pretended it never happened. For everyone watching the Syria campaign unfold, Khasham was the proof that American firepower, when unleashed without political restraint, is still the most lethal force on the planet. The Russians found out the hard way.
belligerents: US Special Operations / SDF / US Air Force
Pro-Assad forces / Russian Wagner Group mercenaries
result: Attacking force destroyed — 100-300+ killed
warName: War against ISIS / Syrian Civil War

name: Death of Zarqawi
description: June 7, 2006. Baqubah, Diyala province. Two F-16s dropped two 500-pound bombs on a safe house outside the city. Inside was Abu Musab al-Zarqawi — the founder of al-Qaeda in Iraq, the architect of the Samarra mosque bombing, the man who personally beheaded hostages on camera, the butcher who turned the Iraqi insurgency into a sectarian apocalypse. JSOC's Task Force 16 had been hunting him for three years, and the intelligence chain that found him was a masterpiece of patient, relentless tradecraft — following courier networks, turning informants, exploiting captured phones and hard drives. When the bombs hit, Delta Force operators moved to the site and found Zarqawi still alive, barely, on a stretcher. He died before he could be treated. His death was celebrated, but the monster he'd created — the ideology, the network, the sectarian war machine — survived him. Al-Qaeda in Iraq became the Islamic State of Iraq, which became ISIS, which conquered half of Iraq and Syria. Zarqawi died, but his war outlived him by a decade.
result: Zarqawi killed — AQI disrupted but not destroyed

name: Fall and Liberation of Ramadi
description: May 2015 — ISIS takes Ramadi. The capital of Anbar province, the city that the Marines and Awakening tribes bled to save in 2006-2007, falls to a force of barely a thousand ISIS fighters using thirty VBIEDs — vehicle-borne IEDs — as rolling artillery. The Iraqi Army's 8th Brigade disintegrates. Soldiers flee in their underwear. The ghosts of every American who fought and died for Ramadi are screaming. Then, December 2015: Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service, backed by coalition airstrikes, begins the liberation. Compound by compound, block by block, through a city ISIS has spent seven months rigging with the most sophisticated IED defenses in the history of warfare. The ICTS — Iraq's Golden Division — takes devastating casualties. But they don't break. They push through. Ramadi is liberated by January 2016. For every OIF veteran, the fall and liberation of Ramadi is the cruelest lesson: you can win a city, hand it to allies, and watch it fall again within a decade. The same real estate, bought twice with blood.
result: Ramadi liberated — city heavily damaged, thousands displaced

name: Liberation of Fallujah from ISIS
description: May-June 2016. Fallujah. Again. Third time. The city that American Marines took in 2004, twice, at the cost of 95 dead and over a thousand wounded, had been under ISIS control since January 2014 — longer than any other Iraqi city. Iraqi security forces and Shia militias surrounded the city and pushed in. The fighting was fierce but the outcome was never really in doubt — by 2016, ISIS was being compressed on all fronts. What was in doubt was what would happen to the Sunni civilians trapped inside. Reports of militia atrocities against fleeing civilians filtered out. Mass detention sites. Disappearances. The sectarian dynamic that had fueled the insurgency in the first place was being replayed under different management. For every Marine who bled in Fallujah's streets in 2004, watching the 2016 liberation was a complicated kind of anguish. The city was being freed, yes. But by forces that might treat its people little better than the occupiers they replaced. Fallujah's curse is that it's always being fought over, and the people who live there always pay the price.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Shia PMF / Coalition air
result: Fallujah liberated — civilian casualties and militia abuses reported

name: Death of Baghdadi (Barisha Raid)
description: October 26, 2019. Barisha, Idlib province, Syria. Delta Force operators fly eight helicopters through some of the most contested airspace on earth — Turkish, Syrian, Russian, and various militia air defense zones — to a compound near the Turkish border. Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, the self-proclaimed Caliph of the Islamic State, the man who stood in the Great Mosque of al-Nuri and declared dominion over every Muslim on earth, runs into a dead-end tunnel with two of his children and detonates a suicide vest. The tunnel collapses. Baghdadi is identified by DNA taken from the remains. A military working dog named Conan pursued him into the tunnel and was injured. The raid was the bookend to a campaign that had consumed five years, involved dozens of nations, and killed hundreds of thousands of people. The caliphate was already rubble. Now its caliph was dead. For every veteran of the ISIS war — for everyone who watched Kirkuk threatened, Mosul fall, Sinjar burn — Baghdadi's death was satisfying in the way that killing a symptom of a disease is satisfying. The man was gone. The conditions that created him were not.
belligerents: US Delta Force / SOAR / US Air Force
Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi / ISIS security detail
result: Baghdadi killed — ISIS leader eliminated
